summary: Reis argues that the belief that AI eliminates the need for learning is dangerously misguided, as our already-shrinking attention spans and surface-level thinking are being exacerbated by AI tools that let people outsource critical thinking. While AI can amplify the capabilities of competent professionals who know what 'good' looks like, it poses significant risks in the hands of those without underlying expertise.
key_points: Surface-level thinking and declining attention spans predate AI but are being accelerated by it
Vibecoded apps may look acceptable on the surface but often contain poorly structured, unmaintainable code
AI amplifies expertise for those who have it, but can do more harm than good for those who don't
We're entering an era of 'AI slopware' - endless disposable, low-quality applications
Expertise and depth should matter more than ever in the AI age, not less
money_quotes: If you're competent, you know what 'good' looks like. If AI does something wrong, you can nudge it along. But AI can do more harm than good in the hands of a rank amateur, especially an incompetent and overconfident person.
We're entering the era of The Great Pacific Garbage Patch of AI Slopware where we churn out endless amounts of disposable and low-quality apps.

What does it mean to be an expert if you're outsourcing your critical thinking and work to an AI? If you don't have sufficient depth in a field or subject, how will you know if AI provides you with the correct answers?
In a time when it's easier than ever to outsource nearly everything to AI, I urge you to take your profession seriously. Enjoying the struggle is what it means to grow as a professional.
key_insight: AI is a force multiplier that amplifies whatever expertise you already have—making competent people more effective while enabling incompetent people to produce higher volumes of low-quality work.
